title: Apple certificates and private keys should not be disclosed
description: Apple push notification certificates, provisioning profiles, and P8/P12 private keys are used to authenticate apps with Apple services including APNs (Apple Push Notification service), App Store Connect API, and code signing. Exposing these credentials enables unauthorized push notifications, app signing, and API access.
recommendation: Remove Apple certificates and private keys from source code. Store P8 key files outside version control, use CI/CD secret management for P12 certificates, and never commit provisioning profiles containing private keys. Revoke exposed certificates in Apple Developer Console.
impact: Unauthorized push notifications to app users, fraudulent app signing, unauthorized App Store Connect API access, and potential app store policy violations.

checks.message: Apple P8 private key (PKCS#8 EC key) detected. Apple P8 private keys are used to authenticate with App Store Connect API, APNs (Apple Push Notification service), and other Apple developer services. Exposing this key allows unauthorized push notifications to your app's users, fraudulent API calls to App Store Connect, and access to app analytics and sales data. Revoke the key in Apple Developer Console immediately at developer.apple.com/account/resources/authkeys.

Apple APNs Key ID detected. APNs key IDs identify the P8 authentication key used to sign push notification requests. While a Key ID alone is not sensitive, it is always found alongside the P8 private key in push notification configurations. This finding indicates an APNs P8 key may be nearby — review surrounding code for the private key content.

Apple Team ID detected in APNs configuration. Apple Team IDs identify your developer account and are used with P8 keys for APNs authentication. Combined with a Key ID and P8 private key (commonly found in the same configuration), these form complete APNs credentials. Ensure no private key is hardcoded in the same file.
